narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 is right around the average for students in our dataset, your family income falls in the middle bracket which is typical, and you're planning to attend full-time like the vast majority—over 92%—of applicants. However, your LSAT score of 30 is notably below the average of about 37, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles both sit at 3, lower than the typical 5-6 range for others. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 41.6%—a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes candidates with stronger predicted odds of bar success. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your undergraduate GPA, which stood strong at average levels and helped offset some concerns. Your middle family income also worked in your favor, suggesting a stable background that typically supports success. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest push toward predicting failure, as being in the lower third of your school's performance stood out as a key risk compared to peers who ranked higher. Your LSAT score added a smaller but still concerning nudge toward failure, given how far it trailed the norm. These top factors—your GPA helping the most, followed by family income, then your Year 3 decile—shaped the model's cautious view of your bar exam readiness relative to typical students. While your full-time status and Year 1 decile offered minor boosts, they weren't enough to tip the scales. This isn't a reflection of your potential or resilience, but a data-driven call based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking targeted academic support to improve your class rankings, and reapplying next cycle with an even stronger profile—many students like you succeed by addressing these exact areas. You've got this; keep pushing.
